---
title: "Your first program"
subtitle: "Edit Leo, watch it compile, run it locally."
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status:** Stub. Full chapter coming soon.
:::

## The question

You've installed Leo. You've run `leo new`. Now what should you actually
write to learn the most in the shortest time?

## What this chapter will cover

A guided "build a token" exercise:

1. **v0** — `add(a, b) -> c` (pure function, no state)
2. **v1** — add a `mapping balances`, write `mint_public`
3. **v2** — add a `record Token`, write `mint_private`
4. **v3** — write `transfer_private`, learn the consume-and-produce pattern
5. **v4** — write `transfer_private_to_public`, the bridge pattern
6. **v5** — add `@test` blocks, run `leo test`

By the end, you've built a 100-line token program that mirrors the structure
of `credits.aleo` and any standalone token contract.

## Interactive viz planned

A side-by-side editor: paste Leo source on the left, see the generated AVM
bytecode on the right, hover any source line to highlight the corresponding
bytecode.

## Why this exercise

You learn more by writing 100 lines of Leo than by reading 1000 lines of docs.
This chapter is paced so each step adds **exactly one** new concept (a
mapping, a record, a transition, etc.) — you can stop at any v.X and have a
working artifact.
